content: Derivatives are financial contracts whose value derives from an underlying asset, index, or rate. The four main types are forwards (customized OTC agreements to buy/sell at future date), futures (standardized exchange-traded forwards), options (right but not obligation to buy/sell), and swaps (exchange of cash flows). Derivatives serve three primary functions: hedging (reducing risk), speculation (taking leveraged positions), and arbitrage (exploiting price discrepancies). The global derivatives market exceeds $600 trillion in notional value, with interest rate swaps being the largest segment. Counterparty risk—the chance the other party defaults—is a key concern, addressed through clearinghouses and collateral requirements post-2008.
